request.question: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?

title: Massachusetts Audubon Society
Pirate Party (Italy)
Jordan River Foundation
Champ de Mars Racecourse
Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre
The Week
Nominative determinism
Gloucester Old Bank
New Scientist
1883 Philadelphia Quakers season
sentences: The Massachusetts Audubon Society, founded in 1896 by Harriet Hemenway and headquartered in Lincoln, Massachusetts, is a nonprofit organization dedicated to "protecting the nature of Massachusetts."
Mass Audubon is independent of the National Audubon Society, and in fact was founded earlier.
Mass Audubon protects 36,500 acres of land throughout Massachusetts, saving birds and other wildlife, and making nature accessible to all with its wildlife sanctuaries and 20 nature centers.
The Italian Pirate Party (Italian: "Partito Pirata Italiano" ) is a political party in Italy, founded on 16 September 2006, modelled on the Pirate Party of Sweden, founded earlier that year.
It supports reform of copyright and patent law, privacy and freedom of expression.

The "Jordan River Foundation" was founded earlier by Queen Nour Al Hussein, the wife of the late king of Jordan Hussein bin Talal, after the death of king Hussein, queen Nour left her position as the Chair person of "Jordan River Foundation" and the new queen Rania took over the position.
The Champ de Mars Racecourse is a thoroughbred horse race track in Port Louis, Mauritius.

The Racecourse was inaugurated on 25 June 1812, by The Mauritius Turf Club (MTC) which was founded earlier in the same year by Sir Robert Townsend Farquhar, who was the first British Governor of Mauritius.
The Mauritius Turf Club is the oldest horse-racing club in the Southern Hemisphere and the second oldest in the world.
The race track follows a very selective right hand oval path and is relatively small in size, with a circumference of 1,298 meters (4,258.5 ft) and width between 12 and 14 meters (39 and 46 feet).
The home-straight extends uphill and is 225 meters (738 ft) long.

The Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre were a Catholic religious order of canons regular of the Rule of Saint Augustine said to have been founded in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, then the capital of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, recognised in 1113 by Papal bull of Pope Paschal II.
Other accounts has it that they were founded earlier, during the rule of Godfrey of Bouillon (1099–1100).
The Week is a weekly British news magazine founded in 1995 which has also published a US edition since 2001 and a children's edition in the UK since 2015.
Between 2008 and 2012 it also published an Australian edition.
Nominative determinism is the hypothesis that people tend to gravitate towards areas of work that fit their names.
The term was first used in the magazine "New Scientist" in 1994, after the magazine's humorous Feedback column noted several studies carried out by researchers with remarkably fitting surnames.
These included a book on polar explorations by Daniel Snowman and an article on urology by researchers named Splatt and Weedon.
These and other examples led to light-hearted speculation that some sort of psychological effect was at work.
Since the term appeared, nominative determinism has been an irregularly recurring topic in "New Scientist", as readers continue to submit examples.
Nominative determinism differs from the related concept aptronym, and its synonyms aptonym, namephreak, and Perfect Fit Last Name, in that it focusses on causality.
"Aptronym" merely means the name is fitting, without saying anything about why it has come to fit.
The Gloucester Old Bank was a British bank that operated between 1716 and 1838.
It was founded in 1716 by James Wood.
The bank was said to have been the oldest private bank in Britain, having survived the financial consequences of the Napoleonic Wars when many other banks went out of business.
The claim was wrong as both C. Hoare & Co and Child & Co. were founded earlier, it was, however, one of the oldest banks in Britain in the nineteenth century.
New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.
Since 1996 it has also run a website.
The 1883 Philadelphia Quakers season was the first in the franchise's existence.
The team was founded earlier in the year as a replacement for the Worcester franchise.
It was the first year Philadelphia was represented in the National League since the original Athletics were disbanded in 1876.
The American Association's Philadelphia Athletics had been founded a year earlier.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?
hop1_docs: New Scientist | New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.  Since 1996 it has also run a website.

The New Times (magazine) | The New Times (Russian: Но́вые Времена́ ) is a Russian language magazine in Russia.  The magazine was founded in 1943.  The current version, established in 1988, , is a liberal, independent Russian weekly news magazine, publishing for Russia and Armenia.  (During the Soviet times it was a multi-language political magazine which followed the official party line.)  Its chief editor is Russian investigative journalist, political scientist, writer and radio host Yevgenia Albats.

Bat Boy (character) | Bat Boy is a creature who made several appearances in the American supermarket tabloid "Weekly World News".  "The Weekly World News" published patently fabricated stories which were purported to be factual.  Within the pages of the paper, Bat Boy is described as a creature who is 'half human and half bat'.  His pursuers, according to "Weekly World News", are scientists and United States government officials; he is frequently captured, then later makes a daring escape.  The original scientist who found him was named Dr. Ron Dillon.  Matthew Daemon, S.O.S. (Seeker of Obscure Supernaturals) crossed paths with him on several occasions.

Vinod Agarwal | Dr. Vinod K. Agarwal is an Indian-American businessman and scientist.  He was a professor at McGill University in Montreal, Canada from 1978 to 1992.  He then founded LogicVision, which he took public in October, 2001, as the first technology IPO following the terrorist attacks earlier that year.  Agarwal left LogicVision in 2005 to start SemIndia, a semiconductor company based in India.

Odisha Sampark Kranti Express | Odisha Sampark Kranti Express is a bi-weekly train which runs between Bhubaneswar,Capital of Odisha and Anand Vihar Terminal.  This train was introduced as part of the Sampark Kranti trains that were announced by the then railway minister Nitish Kumar in 2005.  It was and still is a bi-weekly train.  Now there is a requirement of attaching AC First class berths to this train.  Earlier the train used to terminate at New Delhi railway station (NDLS) but now terminates at Anand Vihar Terminus (ANVT) of New Delhi.  Its rakes are maintained by East Coast Railway (ECoR).

The Aquarian Weekly | The Aquarian Weekly is a regional alternative weekly newspaper based in New Jersey.  Founded in 1969, its focus is rock music and related events in the New Jersey/New York City/Eastern Pennsylvania region.  From 1986 to 1992, it was called East Coast Rocker.  After returning to its original title, the newspaper began including a pull-out section that retained the "East Coast Rocker" name, and which is now freely distributed throughout the region.

New Adventures | New Adventures is a British dance company, specialising in contemporary dance.  Founded by the choreographer Matthew Bourne in 2002, the company developed from an earlier company Adventures in Motion Pictures, which was later dissolved.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] states "New Scientist ... founded in 1956." The passages do not give any founding date for The Week—retrieve The Week's founding year (founding date/publication start) to compare which was founded earlier. Use the phrase "founded" or "first published" for The Week as retrieval anchors.
hop2_docs: Telegraf (Baltimore newspaper) | The Telegraf was a local weekly newspaper published in Baltimore, Maryland.  The newspaper ran for 42 years, from February 20, 1909 until 1951.  It was directed at the Czech community in Baltimore and was published in the Czech language.  The newspaper was founded and first published by Vaclav Joseph Shimek, who also founded the Grand Lodge Č.S.P.S.  of Baltimore.  After 1929, the newspaper was edited by the Rev. Frank Novak and published by August Klecka.

Lake County Examiner | The Lake County Examiner is a weekly newspaper published in Lakeview, Oregon, United States.  The newspaper was founded in 1880 by Stephen P. Moss and Charles A. Cogswell.  Over the years, the paper has had a number of publishers.  Today, it is owned by the Pioneer News Group.  In addition to the weekly newspaper, the "Examiner" staff publishes a number of special editions each year along with a local real estate guide and a twice-weekly news flyer.

Oriental Weekly | Oriental Weekly (東方報) is a Chinese language ethnic weekly newspaper, based in Calgary, Alberta.  It was founded in 1981 to serve the growing Chinese population in Western Canada.  "Oriental Weekly" is owned by Trend Media, parent company of another weekly Chinese newspaper, Trend Weekly (閒情報).  Its publisher is Danny Chan, 30 years veteran of Chinese publishing in Canada.

Princeton Alumni Weekly | The Princeton Alumni Weekly (PAW) is a magazine published for the alumni of Princeton University.  It was founded in 1900 and, until 1977, it was the only weekly college alumni magazine in the United States.  Upon changing to biweekly publication in 1977, the number of issues per year decreased from twenty-eight to twenty-one, and then later decreased to seventeen.  It still remains the most frequently published alumni magazine in the world, currently publishing 14 times per year.

Venetzueler Vochnblat | The Venetzueler Vochnblat (in Yiddish ווענעזועלער וואכנבלאט; in Spanish Semanario Venezolano, "Venezuelan Weekly") was a newspaper founded by Abraham Stempel (who years later founded "Prensa Israelita") in Caracas, Venezuela, in 1959.  It was a tri-lingual paper, being published in Yiddish, Hebrew and Spanish.  Originally it was published weekly but two years after its beginning was changed into a monthly newspaper, until 1964 when it was closed.

Weekly Eleven | Weekly Eleven is a weekly newspaper published in Burma.  It is one of the 5 weekly journals published by Eleven Media Group which was founded in June 2000.  It is focused on general local news and some sports and international news.  It won the "Media of the Year" award in 2011 from Reporters Without Borders, for standing up to the junta and "using extraordinary ingenuity to slip through the censorship net and inform the public".

The Chicago Maroon | The Chicago Maroon, the independent student newspaper of the University of Chicago, is a twice-weekly publication that traces its founding to 1892.  During autumn, winter, and spring quarters of the academic year, the "Maroon" publishes every Tuesday and Friday.  The paper consists of four sections: news, opinion ("Viewpoints"), arts, and sports.  In the late summer, it publishes its annual orientation Issue (O-Issue) for entering first-year students, including sections on the University and the city of Chicago.
response.summary_2: New Scientist was founded in 1956. The supplied passages contain no founding date or identity-aligned information for The Week, so the available evidence does not allow determining which was founded earlier.
response.answer: The supplied passages contain no founding date or identity-aligned information for The Week, so the available evidence does not allow determining which was founded earlier.
